creator: list1@gjunka.com
text:
(In reply to Brooks Davis from comment #3)
HIPE compiler can't fall back to non-LLVM backend without user intervention. What happens is that you pass an option to the compiler to use LLVM backend. See: https://erllvm.softlab.ntua.gr/

For example:

2> c(test, [native, {hipe, [to_llvm]}]).
{ok,test}

Then HIPE checks if the correct version of LLVM is available. If not, it shows an error so user has to use non-LLVM backend. An application would do it automatically based on the error returned.

I don't know how exactly LLVM backend is used in HIPE but my guess is that it's very limited and doesn't depend on an exact version of LLVM. And I doubt that they would like to push to Erlang main code changes specific to FreeBSD to use the llvm** suffixes. It would need to be either:
1. A change maintained in the Erlang port for FreeBSD to patch the Erlang code with the proper suffix.
2. A change in the LLVM port to allow installing non-suffixed binaries

First option is not good because every time a new version of LLVM is installed Erlang port would need to be recompiled (which would be an overkill considering that LLVM is optional and the Erlang code is already checking the version of the installed LLVM).

Second option is in my opinion much more viable because it would solve the problem in a more universal way, not only for Erlang but also for other scripts or applications, and for the command line if someone just wants to play with LLVM and doesn't want to install more than one version at a time.

How about a configuration option to automatically add ${LOCALBASE}/llvm##/bin to $PATH (if possible)? Or to install the binaries (or create links) without suffixes in addition to the suffixed versions? In the later case the non-suffixed version would be the latest installed (with a warning if existing links/binaries have been overwritten).
